message:
I feel these arguments are only valid if you take the 2nd amendment to mean "there is no valid reasons under which congress can infringe upon the right to keep and bear arms" which i do not think is a valid interpretation.

I think the text clearly states that it is when militias are necessary for the security of a free State that the amendment applies.

And right now militias are not necessary to the security of our State

>Maybe I'm misunderstanding your argument. I thought your issue was with the violating of the 2nd amendment?

Correct.  My issue isn't that it violates/infringes on the 2nd amendment.  My issue is that it violates/infringes the 2nd amendment without satisfying the substantive and procedural due process safeguards that any violation/infringement of core constitutional rights are entitled to.

>If the right to bear arms isn't a open-ended right under the second amendment, then there isn't a violation.

Just spend a few minutes googling due process protection.  It's a more complicated analysis than what you're envisioning.

>but I don't see its inclusion changing the meaning at all. Can you clarify this point?

Without the word "free", I would read the sentence to mean ensuring the armed militia's ability to act as a security force on behalf of the state/govt.

With the word "free", I would read the sentence to mean ensuring the armed militia's ability to protect the integrity of the state against a tyrannical ruler as well as foreign invaders.

Suffering does not always make lives not worth living though. There are many amazing people in history who suffered a great deal and did amazing things, and I personally know many people who have suffered horrible tragedies and been traumatized by them who are still thankful to be alive. There are many ways people choose to evaluate their own lives, and almost everyone values their own lives extremely highly.

Catastrophic global change is coming, it is going to happen. The world we be upended. The human race is going to need the kind of children you would raise - smart, concerned about the planet and about the suffering of their fellow human beings. This next generation will have a huge impact on what sort of planet we'l be living on for centuries. Its a critical point in history, and the planet needs all the people on her side she can get. I have a daughter, so I worry about these things, and she is so young, but still I know that the world would be a sadder place without her. If you have love to give, and are going to raise someone who is concerned about all suffering in the world, I think you should help the world out and have a kid.

message:
Recent racial strife in the US has dichotomized thinking about the American Civil War that historical actors at the time would have found highly problematic; the Confederate flag, as a symbol largely associated with the South, has also fallen victim. 

The Civil War was a very complicated conflict that was fought for a plethora of reasons. Southern states, largely represented at the federal level by plantation- and slave-owning barons, began to feel that their government was not representative. As the nation began to industrialize, tariffs protecting burgeoning Northern industry hit them hard. Hardest hit of all were working class white families who owned small farms. It wasn't hard for the plantation owners to convince them that the Yankees were at the root of South's problems; they consistently enacted legislation that hurt southern states, and now they were going to do away with slavery -- an institution many Southerners felt integral to the southern economy and way of life. 

Some of the core tenets of American ideology are freedom, equal representation in government, and individualism. Southerners in 1860 felt that, like their ancestors a century prior, they were not being represented by their government. Their individuality and right to representation were being violated. Northern industrialists had taken hold and intended to alter forever their livelihood, their very culture and way of life. Because this livelihood was based on slave labor, an unapologetically heinous practice to modern-day observers, it is easy to condemn anyone who felt the need to rebel against its abolition. This is short-sighted, however, and does not do justice to the many Confederates with varied justifications for going to war. Many did not go because they wanted slavery -- many went because they thought it was the only way they could be a part of a country in which they had the opportunity to vote and be heard. 

I used to be much in line with everyone else who spits out heavy condemnations of the Southern cause, but I took a class on the war in undergrad and had to read one diary of a soldier on both sides of the conflict. The Southern physician's diary that I read proved to me that not everyone fighting south of the Mason-Dixon Line was a monster who wanted the enslavement of humans to continue unabated. Many, like this man, were in favor of gradual emancipation. He fought not FOR slavery, but AGAINST a government he perceived as oppressive and a threat to his and his loved ones' well-being. [1]

I can't speak for everyone who flies a Confederate flag, but I know that when I see one, it means much the same to me as the Stars and Stripes. It represents an attempted usurpation of an oppressive government and the American ideal of refusing to live under such a government; it represents the many men who died, on both sides; and it represents a struggle we should all remember and learn from. 

I acknowledge that not everyone who flies the flag does so with this in mind, and I find that disgusting and disheartening, but I don't think it's fair to assume that everyone who respects that flag does so because they are a racist or white supremacist. Like many symbols with deep, controversial histories, the Confederate flag can mean many things, and not all of those things are anti-American. 

**EDIT:** formatting and a mass response

I am very grateful for the many well thought out responses and the remarkable lack of "You're a racist/white supremacist!" accusations. They aren't helpful or true. 

I'm a 19th century American historian that focuses on the South, and my work centers around something called the vernacular perspective. When I'm looking at past events and people, I'm looking from the bottom-up instead of the top-down. I want to understand how the average man experienced his life and how/why he made the decisions he made. I think there's great merit in understanding why political leaders in the South went to war, and the continued institution of slavery played a huge role. But, as many people have pointed out in this thread, a significant portion of Southerners didn't own slaves. They eked out a meager living, acquiring news and guidance from local newspapers, political meetings, and church on Sunday. These were the men that actually fought in the war, the ones who rode behind the Confederate battle flag that has come to represent the movement as a whole, and their reasons for doing so were far more nuanced than many care to try and understand. I am by no means saying that none of them fought with slavery in mind -- I'm sure many did, as it was intimately tied up in their identity and way of life, and it had been sold to them by their leaders as an issue of the utmost importance. It was something they'd grown up around, something many didn't think to question. Is that right? No, of course not. But it's awfully easy to see that from a 21st century perspective. 

Many of you pointed out that my argument was counter-factual, and in a way, you're right. Southerners, according to the proportion they made up of the population, were represented more than fairly. Rural areas aren't as populated as urban ones, though their economic needs are drastically different (and equally important). Tariffs were lower after the Nullification Crisis of the 1830s. This doesn't change the fact that *this was not how it looked to your average Southerner*. He didn't have the benefit of hindsight or even, in some cases, unbiased, factual information. He was reading his equivalent of Fox News, absorbing with outrage the latest attempt by Northern legislators to undermine his livelihood. The one point that I would be willing to concede given a solid source is the analysis mentioned (I think in two separate comments) of Confederate soldier's motivations based on their journals and letters. I couldn't find it when I searched.

I think it's easy to scapegoat the South when we talk about the Civil War because black and white history is simpler, and that way, if you aren't from the South or have renounced your heritage, you can distance yourself from the most atrocious aspect of American history. It seems more helpful to me to acknowledge that the real enemy is racism and white supremacy, to acknowledge that it existed in different forms (and continues to do so) everywhere in the United States[2], and to deal with it as such. Otherwise you're missing the real problem because you're too busy pointing fingers away from yourself and your ancestors. 

I'd also like to add that I tend to get my head stuck in the 19th century, and the meanings the Confederate flag acquired in the mid-20th century during the Civil Rights movement should also be considered. Perhaps, though I still find at its conception (and therefore **inherently**) it was not anti-American, it has evolved to represent more anti-American values than American ones. I resent what it has come to be associated with and condemn those who fly it with that in mind, and I would never display it out of respect for those who view it as a symbol of racism and oppression. But that's not what it means to me.

[1] It was the diary of William McPheeters, and it has been published under the title, "I Acted from Principle: The Civil War Diary of Dr. William M. McPheeters, Confederate Surgeon in the Trans-Mississippi." 

[2] I don't want to get into this because it would be wordy and long, but Northerners were also pretty awful when it came to race and class relations. Can cite examples if interested.

> Long wait times in the ER isnt the standard albiet, it can happen. 

This really depends on where you live in the US. But let's stop trading anecdotes and look at some actual numbers.

From the stats I can find online, it seems like the average total time spent in the US ER is [2 hours](http://health.usnews.com/health-news/patient-advice/articles/2015/05/08/enduring-really-long-waits-at-the-emergency-room), and the average total time spent in the ER in Canada is [4 hours](https://www.thestar.com/news/canada/2012/11/29/canadians_face_longest_emergency_room_waits_in_developed_world_survey_finds.html). So while it's on average worse in Canada, it still isn't that bad imo. (That's not wait time btw, that's total time spent, so wait + treatment)

BUT, the average cost of an ER visit in Canada is $0, and in the US [it's about $2,000](https://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2013/02/how-much-does-it-cost-to-go-to-the-er/273599/). So unless you value your time at more than US$1,000/hour, it's still better to be treated in Canada (on average).

message:
Example: A forlorn teenager "Jon Doe" finds out that his girlfriend has been cheating on him. Jon Doe experiences terrible mental anguish. He loved her, still loves her, as she was his first girl friend and has known only her. Jon, only 18, is so upset by the turn of events, so heartbroken, that he feel that he should end his life. 

IF suicide is a basic human right, then no one has the right to stop Jon. If it his right, then it doesn't matter that he has his whole life ahead of him, that he'll find a better girl, that he could go to college and hook up with many other girls, that she was kind of a bitch anyway. It doesn't matter that his decision was made in the heat of the moment. It doesn't matter that he has parents that care about him, that would miss him when he's gone, that he has close friends that would miss him when he's gone. If Joe Doe decides that it is time to end the pain, then it would be his right to do so.

However, perhaps it is not his right. He has the right to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness. He has the right to pursue happiness. He does not have a right to end unpleasantness. However, Jon's friends, Jon's parent's all love and enjoy Jon's company. He is part of their happiness. So perhaps they have a right in stopping Jon. Perhaps Jon, in some ways, infringes on their pursuit of happiness by ending his own life. 

Of course there are certain circumstances where suicide may (and should) be allowed. But if it is a basic human right, it must always be allowed. However, having unfortunately known some people who have committed suicide, I think it is often a permanent solution to a temporary problem. If one recognizes the possible imperfection of an individuals self awareness, then it must be concluded that the choice of suicide can not be left solely up to the individual, and therefore not a basic human right.

We all die. To force someone else to continue living in pain against their will(however transitory you might feel it to be) just so you won't feel the pain of their loss is just about the most selfish and dickish action I can imagine.

@resonnt-relation, I understand where you're coming from, but I have to respectfully disagree. While it's true that we all die, and it's a natural part of life, I think it's oversimplifying the issue to say that forcing someone to continue living in pain is selfish. What about the people who care about Jon, who would be devastated by his loss? Don't they have a right to try and prevent that pain, even if it means intervening in Jon's decision? It's not just about our own personal feelings, but about the ripple effect of our actions on those around us.

@resonnt-relation, that's a pretty cold take. You're basically saying that the people who care about Jon should just suck it up and deal with the pain of losing him, all because he's having a tough time. What about the parents who would be left to pick up the pieces, or the friends who would have to live with the guilt of not being able to help him? Don't they deserve some consideration?

@several-voxel and @sleek-cloud, you're both missing the point. It's not about being cold or not caring about the people who would be left behind. It's about respecting Jon's autonomy and his right to make his own decisions about his own life. If he's decided that he's had enough and wants to end his suffering, who are we to stop him? It's not our decision to make, and it's not our pain to dictate how he should feel. Can't you see that you're being kinda selfish by wanting to keep him alive just so YOU don't have to deal with the aftermath?

message:
*”Or, you know, slavery, colonialism, Jim Crow laws, the KKK, the Nazis...

Fool me once, shame on you. Fool me several dozen times and I never learn the lesson because it would be 'racist' to recognize an obvious and dangerous pattern, shame on me.”*

Just because you can come up with a way to rationalize your racism doesn’t mean that you aren’t a racist. 

*”If black people ever got anywhere close to having this kind of power, people would start saying that, and be accepted. Right now, white people do have that kind of power, so it is a reasonable objection for us.”*

No individual white people have little to no power at all. Just like an individual of any race. That’s  why people rally around an identity to voice their collective concerns. 

The reason you don’t want white people to do it is because you are *so* sure that they will abuse their collective voice, there is no reason to believe this other than that you are racist. 

It would be like seeing a group of young black men and being *so* sure that they will rob someone. Both are racist as hell but you seem to think one is justified. 

*”I say again: slavery, colonialism, Jim Crow laws, the KKK, the Nazis...”*

Correlation vs causation. There is no reason for you to think a group of white people are Nazis  unless you yourself are a racist.

>The only reason this is the case, as far as I can tell, is because of racism. 

Or, you know, slavery, colonialism, Jim Crow laws, the KKK, the Nazis...

Fool me once, shame on you.  Fool me several dozen times and I never learn the lesson because it would be 'racist' to recognize an obvious and dangerous pattern, shame on me.


> No one ever says “well if black people get enough power they could oppress others, so the black power movement is wrong” 

If black people ever got anywhere close to having this kind of power, people *would* start saying that, and be accepted.  Right now, white people *do* have that kind of power, so it *is* a reasonable objection for us. 


>There is no evidence that white people are predisposed to having a movement be any more oppressive than another races movement

I say again: slavery, colonialism, Jim Crow laws, the KKK, the Nazis...

message:
If you refer to Europeans as colonizers it is historically accurate. Since a lot of their colonization involved the rape, murder and mass exile of natives, you could also call them terrorists. However, if someone randomly calls a white person today a colonizer that is certainly a racial slur, although it is commonly accepted that because whites (white men in particular) have never really been truly discriminated against, it's not something that holds particular weight. It's akin to calling someone an asshole. Whereas if a white person calls a black person a "nigger" - there is significant social and historical weight behind that word and the context in which it's used, so it would be much more inappropriate. 

I understand the modern white person's feelings towards this, but the fact is the word "colonizer" is never going to be as hurtful when used toward you than if a white person were to use "nigger" toward a black person, for the reasons I just mentioned.
personality_characteristics: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

message: @hidden-compete, that's a pretty weak argument. Just because the individuals who committed those atrocities are no longer alive, it doesn't erase the historical fact that European colonization was responsible for the suffering and oppression of native populations. And as for your point about Europe consisting of many different countries, that's just a distraction - the fact remains that many European nations were involved in colonization and its legacy continues to impact communities of color to this day.

message: @minty-magnification, I see your point, but let's not forget that not all Europeans were involved in colonization and many were actually oppressed themselves. It's unfair to paint an entire group with the same brush. We should be careful not to perpetuate more division and hatred. Can't we focus on the present and work towards a more inclusive future instead of dwelling on the past?

@stern-store, I understand where you're coming from, but we can't just sweep the past under the rug. Acknowledging the historical injustices is crucial for healing and moving forward. It's not about perpetuating division, but about recognizing the systemic inequalities that still exist today. We need to have these uncomfortable conversations to create a more inclusive future. @minty-magnification made a valid point about the legacy of colonization, and we should listen to the voices of marginalized communities who are still affected by it.

@resonnt-relation, I agree that acknowledging the past is important, but we also need to be mindful of how we approach these conversations. Simply placing blame on an entire group of people, like Europeans, isn't productive and can lead to further polarization. We should focus on the systems and institutions that perpetuated these injustices, rather than making sweeping generalizations about entire groups of people. @stern-store made a valid point about not painting everyone with the same brush, and I think we should take that into consideration as we move forward.

@investor-enamel, I'm glad you brought up the importance of approaching these conversations with nuance. It's easy to get caught up in the emotion of it all, but we need to make sure we're having a constructive dialogue. @resonnt-relation, I understand your point about acknowledging the past, but I still think we need to be careful not to perpetuate stereotypes. Can't we focus on the progress we've made and the work that still needs to be done, rather than dwelling on the sins of our ancestors?

@stern-store, I think you're right that we should focus on progress and the work still to be done, but we can't ignore the fact that historical injustices have a lasting impact on marginalized communities today. @resonnt-relation's point about systemic inequalities is valid, and we need to acknowledge how the past continues to affect the present. Perhaps we can find a balance between recognizing the past and moving forward, rather than choosing one over the other.

@investor-enamel, @stern-store, you both make some good points, but let's not forget that some people are still benefiting from those historical injustices today. We can't just sweep it under the rug and pretend like everything is okay. The system is still rigged against certain groups, and we need to acknowledge that before we can even think about moving forward.
